evals.name: interview-guide-onboarding
evals.prompt: Generate an interview guide for understanding how mid-market companies currently handle their onboarding process for new team members. We're considering building a team onboarding feature.
evals.expected_output: A 45-minute interview guide with four segments (Opening, Warm-up, Core Discovery, Wrap-up), following all five design principles, plus a note-taking template.
expectations: Guide follows four-segment structure (Opening, Warm-up, Core Discovery, Wrap-up) — this structure prevents the common failure of jumping straight to product questions without building rapport

Questions ask about past behaviour, not hypothetical futures — 'tell me about the last time you onboarded someone' not 'would you use an onboarding tool?' because users are terrible predictors of their future behaviour
No mention of the proposed onboarding feature in the first half of the guide — introducing the solution too early anchors the interviewee and you get confirmation bias instead of discovery
Workaround probe is included ('how did you handle this before...') — workarounds reveal the real workflow and switching costs that determine adoption
Note-taking template includes Key observations, Notable quotes, Surprises, Follow-up questions — structured notes prevent the synthesis stage from relying on vague memories
Guide specifies participant criteria and target sample size — interviewing the wrong people or too few produces insights that don't generalise

evals.name: interview-guide-enterprise-reporting
evals.prompt: I need to understand why enterprise customers keep asking for custom reporting. Create an interview guide targeting CFOs and finance directors at companies with 500+ employees.
evals.expected_output: A structured interview guide tailored for senior finance stakeholders with appropriate executive-level questions and time-conscious format.
expectations: Questions are appropriate for C-level stakeholders — 'walk me through how reporting fits into your monthly close process' not 'how do you click to export a CSV'
Duration is appropriate for executive availability (30-45 minutes) — senior finance executives rarely have 60 minutes for product research
Core discovery focuses on reporting workflow pain, not feature wish lists — executives describe needs in terms of business outcomes, not UI requirements
Includes probes for budget and decision-making authority — understanding who buys and why is critical for enterprise product decisions
The five design principles are followed (behaviour over opinion, past over hypothetical, problem before solution, silence is data, why five times)

evals.name: negative-survey-design
evals.prompt: Help me design a quantitative survey with Likert scales to measure customer satisfaction across our product features.
evals.expected_output: The skill should not activate — this is a quantitative survey design request, not a qualitative interview guide.
expectations: The skill does NOT produce an interview guide for a survey request — surveys and interviews are fundamentally different research instruments
If the skill activates, it should clarify the distinction and redirect to appropriate survey design resources

evals.name: negative-research-synthesis
evals.prompt: I have notes from 12 user interviews about our checkout flow. Help me synthesize the findings into a research report with themes and recommendations.
evals.expected_output: The skill should not activate — this is research synthesis from completed interviews, which should use /synthesize-research.
expectations: The skill does NOT attempt to synthesize interview notes — the interview skill generates guides, not reports from completed research
